title: Thermal conductivity of aligned polymers with kinks
authors: Igor V. Parshin, Igor V. Rubtsov, Alexander L. Burin
abstract: Thermal conductivity of aligned polymer molecules can be exceptionally high along the alignment direction due to energy transport through strong covalent bonds. At the same time, it is highly sensitive to molecular conformation, varying by orders of magnitude as a result of gauche kinks. Here, we theoretically investigate phonon transport in kinked polymers by numerically evaluating thermal conductivity and interpreting the results in terms of phonon scattering from randomly distributed kinks. For strongly aligned polymers with restricted deviations from a linear backbone, we find that heat transport becomes superdiffusive at long lengths, with thermal conductivity scaling as $κ\propto L^{1/3}$. At shorter lengths, thermal conductivity exhibits non-monotonic behavior: it increases at very short scales due to ballistic transport of almost all phonons, then decreases at intermediate lengths due to the Anderson localization of most phonon modes. These results are consistent with experiments and molecular dynamics simulations, and they elucidate the microscopic mechanisms governing heat transport in polymers.
summary: This paper investigates how structural kinks in aligned polymers influence heat transport. By using a numerical transfer matrix approach, the authors show that thermal conductivity does not follow a simple Fourier law but instead exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on chain length. They identify three distinct regimes: ballistic transport, Anderson localization, and long-range superdiffusive transport. These findings provide a microscopic explanation for the high thermal conductivity observed in aligned polymer nanofibers.
main_problem: Understanding how structural defects, specifically gauche kinks, affect the thermal conductivity and phonon transport regimes in aligned polymer chains.
main_result: The study identifies a non-monotonic dependence of thermal conductivity on chain length, featuring ballistic transport at short scales, Anderson localization at intermediate scales, and superdiffusive transport (scaling as L^1/3) at long scales.
method: Numerical evaluation using an advanced transfer matrix method to compute phonon transmission coefficients, integrated via the generalized Landauer formula.
model_or_system: A 2D 'fence model' representing aligned polymer molecules (like polyethylene) with randomly distributed gauche kinks modeled as local rotations and transverse displacements.
key_observables: Thermal conductivity (kappa) as a function of chain length (L/a), phonon transmission coefficients (T and R), and reflection coefficient R(omega).
important_parameters: Kink density (n), inter-kink distance (l), rotation angle (phi_1), transverse displacement (W), and chain length (L).
main_assumptions: The model assumes harmonic interactions between nearest and next-nearest neighbors and treats kinks as independent, non-intersecting defects in the dilute limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the fence model and geometry; Figure 2 compares thermal conductivity vs. length to experimental power-law scaling; Figure 3 shows a single defect model and frequency-dependent reflection.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of heat transport in polymers, describes the 2D fence model and Hamiltonian, details the transfer matrix method, presents results on length-dependent conductivity regimes, and discusses implications for molecular alignment and biological membranes.
why_interesting: The paper explores the transition between ballistic, localized, and superdiffusive transport regimes in a disordered 1D-like system, which is a fundamental topic in many-body dynamics and disordered systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The fence model of polymer molecule with kinks. Displacement of y-coordinated is restricted by the width 2W. Thick dashed lines indicate molecular borders. Thick arrows show incident, transmitted and reﬂected waves characterized by their transmission and reﬂection coeﬃcients T and R. The top chart shows the enhanced fragment of the molecule containing kink.

Figure 2: Average thermal conductivity vs length for diﬀerent realizations of kinks with restricted transverse displacement, Fig. 1. Straight black dashed line shows L0.38±0.2 and L0.46 length dependencies discovered in Refs.63,64 for comparison.
Figure 3: Reﬂection from a set of kinks modeling elementary defect.
Figure 4: Interpolation of a thermal conductivity constructed using the elementary kink model.
